era_context: A pivotal year in ancient history. The Old Kingdom of Egypt is collapsing or has just collapsed; Pepi II's death (or the end of his reign) marks the transition to the First Intermediate Period. Akkad remains powerful but is approaching its own collapse. The Harappan civilization is stable. This year represents a threshold between two eras in Egypt and Mesopotamia.

events.title: Death of Pepi II and collapse of Old Kingdom centralization
events.description: Pepi II's death (or the end of his reign) marks the effective end of the Old Kingdom and the beginning of the First Intermediate Period (c. 2184–2055 BCE). The centralized authority of the pharaoh collapses rapidly. Provincial governors (nomarchs) assert independence, and Egypt fragments into competing regional powers. The transition is not marked by a single catastrophic event but by a gradual erosion of central authority that accelerates after Pepi II's death. The First Intermediate Period is characterized by political fragmentation, reduced monumental construction, and the rise of provincial elites.
key_figures: Pepi II, last pharaoh of Dynasty VI (or one of the last)
Merenre II and other ephemeral successors

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of reduced monumental construction

events.certainty_note: The collapse of the Old Kingdom is well-attested in the archaeological record and later king lists. However, the exact date of Pepi II's death is disputed. Traditional chronology places it c. 2184 BCE, but some scholars propose dates up to 100 years earlier or later. -2135 is assigned based on conventional dating, but uncertainty of ±50 years is reasonable.
cross_references: Marks the beginning of the First Intermediate Period (c. -2184 to -2055)
Contemporary with or slightly precedes the collapse of Akkad

events.title: Naram-Sin of Akkad: empire faces critical challenges
events.description: Naram-Sin's empire is under severe strain. Rebellions in conquered territories are frequent; the Gutians are pressing from the northeast; and internal dissent is growing. Some scholars interpret later chronicles as suggesting that Naram-Sin's reign is ending or has recently ended by this time, though the exact date is uncertain. The empire is approaching its collapse, which will occur within the next 15–20 years. The combination of external military pressure and internal rebellion will prove fatal to Akkadian centralization.

sources.name: Later chronicles describing the Gutian invasion

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but the exact end date is debated. Some scholars place his death c. 2218 BCE, others c. 2200 BCE or later. -2135 may fall within his reign or shortly after his death, depending on the chronology used. Uncertainty of ±20 years is reasonable.
cross_references: Precedes the Gutian invasion and collapse of Akkad (c. -2154 to -2150)

events.title: Harappan civilization: continued mature phase
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Harappan civilization continues in its mature phase, unaffected by the transformations occurring in Egypt and Mesopotamia. Urban centers remain prosperous, trade networks are extensive, and material culture shows continuity. Archaeological evidence reveals no signs of disruption at this time. The civilization will remain stable for another 200+ years.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's stability during the mature phase is well-established through archaeology.
cross_references: Part of the mature Harappan phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pepi II's death and the beginning of the First Intermediate Period is highly disputed. Some scholars place the Old Kingdom's collapse as early as -2250 or as late as -2100, a range of 150 years. The exact dating of Naram-Sin's reign and death is also debated, with some scholars proposing dates significantly earlier or later than traditional chronology. The interpretation of the 'Gutian invasion' as a sudden military conquest is questioned by some scholars, who suggest a more gradual process of infiltration and political change. The Harappan civilization's apparent stability may mask environmental or social stresses not visible in the archaeological record.
historiographic_note: This year marks a major transition in the historical record. The collapse of the Old Kingdom in Egypt and the impending collapse of Akkad represent the end of two major centralized states. However, the exact dating of these events is uncertain, with chronologies differing by decades or even centuries. Egyptian sources are biased toward royal and elite perspectives; the experiences of common people during the transition are largely invisible. Mesopotamian sources are similarly elite-focused. The Indus Valley Civilization's lack of deciphered writing means that understanding is based entirely on material culture. The synchronization of events across these three civilizations is uncertain; they may not have occurred in the same year despite being assigned to the same conventional date.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's death initiates the First Intermediate Period of Egyptian fragmentation
Naram-Sin's empire will collapse within ~15–20 years
Two major civilizations (Egypt and Akkad) are undergoing simultaneous or near-simultaneous collapse, though the exact synchronization is uncertain
